walkthrough.title: Grab the Essentials
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Just One. Each player takes one easel and one erasable felt marker. The easels are dual-purpose: they hold your clue so others can see it and they display the mystery word card for the active player. Make sure everyone has a working marker.

walkthrough.title: Build the Session Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all one hundred and ten word cards and draw exactly thirteen. Place them face down in a pile in the center of the table. Put the rest back in the box. These thirteen cards are your entire game. Each card has five numbered words, so there are sixty-five possible mystery words in your session.

walkthrough.title: Three-Player Adjustment
walkthrough.text: If you have exactly three players, each person takes two easels instead of one. During the clue phase, each player writes two clues. This gives the guesser more information to work with. For four to seven players, stick with one easel and one clue per person.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Active Player
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to be the first active player. This person will guess the mystery word while everyone else writes clues. The active player role rotates to the left after each turn. There is no advantage to going first, so just pick someone and start.

walkthrough.title: How a Turn Begins
walkthrough.text: The active player draws the top card from the pile without looking at the front. They place the card in their easel so all the other players can read the five numbered words. The active player then calls out a number from one to five. That word becomes the mystery word for this turn. Everyone is ready to write clues. Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Grab the Essentials
bullets: Each player takes 1 easel + 1 erasable marker
Easels hold clues and display the mystery word card

summary.title: Build the Session Deck
bullets: Shuffle 110 word cards, draw exactly 13 for this game
Return the rest to the box
Each card has 5 numbered words

summary.title: Three-Player Adjustment
bullets: 3 players: each takes 2 easels and writes 2 clues per turn
4-7 players: 1 easel and 1 clue per person

summary.title: Choose the First Active Player
bullets: Active player guesses the word; everyone else writes clues
Role rotates left after each turn

summary.title: How a Turn Begins
bullets: Active player draws top card without looking, places in easel
Calls out a number 1-5 → that word is the mystery word
Other players can now read all 5 words

walkthrough.title: The Goal
walkthrough.text: Just One is a cooperative word game. Your team works together to guess as many mystery words as possible out of thirteen cards. There is no competition. Everyone wins or loses together based on how many cards you get right. A perfect score is thirteen. Anything above nine is a great result.

walkthrough.title: Writing Clues
walkthrough.text: When the active player calls a number, all other players secretly write one clue word on their easel. You must write only one word. No table talk, no gesturing, no eye contact to coordinate. Each player comes up with their clue independently. You want your clue to help the guesser figure out the mystery word.

walkthrough.title: What Counts as a Valid Clue
walkthrough.text: Your clue must be a single word. Numbers, acronyms, and onomatopoeia are allowed. But you cannot use the mystery word in a different form, like adding a suffix or prefix. You cannot translate the mystery word into another language. You cannot use a word from the same word family. And you cannot make up a word. If your clue is invalid, it gets cancelled.

walkthrough.title: Comparing Clues
walkthrough.text: After everyone writes their clue, the active player closes their eyes. The clue-givers reveal their easels to each other. Now comes the critical step: check for duplicates. If two or more players wrote the same word, or words that are close variants like plurals, gender forms, or different spellings, all matching clues are cancelled. Tip the cancelled easels face down.

walkthrough.title: The Guess
walkthrough.text: The active player opens their eyes and sees only the surviving clues. They get exactly one guess. Just one. If they guess correctly, the card goes to the success pile. If they guess wrong, the card is discarded and you also lose the top card of the deck, which is a harsh penalty. The active player may also choose to skip instead of guessing.

walkthrough.title: Skipping Versus Guessing Wrong
walkthrough.text: If the active player is not confident, they can skip. Skipping only costs the current card. Guessing wrong costs the current card plus the top card of the deck. That is two cards lost instead of one. In the late game when every card counts, skipping a shaky guess is often the smart play.

walkthrough.title: All Clues Cancelled
walkthrough.text: Sometimes every single clue gets cancelled because of duplicates. When this happens, the active player does not get to guess at all. The card is simply removed from the game. No extra penalty beyond losing the card. This is why writing unique clues is so important.

walkthrough.title: Final Turn Special Rule
walkthrough.text: On the very last card, there is an extra penalty for guessing wrong. In addition to losing the current card, you also remove one previously successful card from your score pile. This makes the final turn especially tense. If you are not confident, skip rather than risk losing two points on the last card.

bullets: Cooperative word game — team tries to guess 13 mystery words
Score = number of correct guesses
13 = perfect, 11+ = awesome, 9-10 = great

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Just One
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will play through several turns with five players. You will see how clue writing, duplicate cancellation, and guessing work together. This is a cooperative game, so everyone is on the same team. Let's try to get a perfect score.

walkthrough.title: Turn One: The Mystery Word
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is the active player. They draw a card, place it in their easel without looking, and call out number three. The other players see the word: Penguin. Now everyone except Player 1 needs to write a one-word clue that will help Player 1 guess Penguin.

walkthrough.title: Writing Clues
walkthrough.text: The four clue-givers write secretly. Player 2 writes Antarctica. Player 3 writes Tuxedo. Player 4 writes Antarctica. Player 5 writes Waddle. Everyone reveals their easels to each other while Player 1 keeps their eyes closed.

walkthrough.title: Duplicate Cancellation
walkthrough.text: Both Player 2 and Player 4 wrote Antarctica. Those clues are duplicates, so both get cancelled. The easels are tipped face down. Player 1 opens their eyes and sees only two surviving clues: Tuxedo and Waddle. Player 1 thinks for a moment and guesses Penguin. Correct! The card goes to the success pile.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Obvious Clue Spam
walkthrough.text: Notice what happened. Two out of four clue-givers wrote the most obvious clue, Antarctica, and both got cancelled. This is the number one mistake in Just One. When everyone writes the first word that pops into their head, duplicates are almost guaranteed. The team lost two clues and got lucky that the remaining two were enough.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Write Your Second Thought
walkthrough.text: The golden rule of Just One: think of the most obvious clue, and then do not write it. Someone else will almost certainly write that one. Instead, go for your second or third association. A slightly less obvious clue that survives is far more valuable than the perfect clue that gets cancelled. Think about what makes the word unique from a different angle.

walkthrough.title: Turn Two: A Tough Word
walkthrough.text: Player 2 is now the active player. The mystery word is Mercury. This is tricky because Mercury can mean the planet, the element, or the Roman god. The clue-givers need to agree on an interpretation without talking to each other.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Mixed Meanings
walkthrough.text: Player 3 writes Planet. Player 4 writes Thermometer. Player 5 writes Freddie. Player 1 writes Orbit. Player 2 opens their eyes and sees four clues pointing in three different directions: planets, temperature, and the singer Freddie Mercury. Player 2 is confused and guesses Thermometer, which is wrong. The card is lost plus the top card of the deck. Two cards gone.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Read the Room
walkthrough.text: With ambiguous words, most clue-givers will gravitate toward the most common meaning. In this case, the planet was the strongest association. If you think most people will write planet-related clues, write something related but different, like Solar or Venus. If you go against the grain entirely, like Freddie, you risk confusing the guesser. Stay in the same lane as your teammates.

walkthrough.title: Turn Three: The Skip Decision
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is the active player. The mystery word is Accordion. After duplicate cancellation, only one clue survives: Squeezebox. Player 3 is not sure what Squeezebox refers to. They could guess, but if they are wrong, they lose this card plus the next card from the deck. Player 3 decides to skip. Only this card is lost. Smart play when unsure.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Know When to Skip
walkthrough.text: Skipping costs one card. Guessing wrong costs two. In the early game, you can afford to take risks because you have many cards left. But as the deck shrinks, the penalty for a wrong guess gets proportionally worse. A good rule of thumb: if you are less than fifty percent confident, skip. Protecting your score pile is more important than gambling for one more point.

walkthrough.title: A Perfect Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 4 is the active player. The mystery word is Lighthouse. Player 5 writes Beacon. Player 1 writes Coastal. Player 2 writes Tower. Player 3 writes Maritime. No duplicates! Player 4 sees all four clues and immediately guesses Lighthouse. Perfect turn. When every clue is unique and complementary, the guesser has all the information they need.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You saw how duplicates kill your best clues, how ambiguous words can confuse the guesser, and when skipping is smarter than guessing. Remember: write your second thought, stay in the same lane as your teammates, and know when to skip.

walkthrough.title: Before You Write
walkthrough.text:
Before you play a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for clue-writing strategies and group coordination.

Second, reference the Appendix for the complete duplicate and invalid clue rules.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the most common mistakes in Just One. First, writing the most obvious clue that everyone else will also write. Second, using invalid clues like word-family variants or translations. Third, being too clever with obscure clues that nobody understands. Fourth, guessing too quickly without considering all the surviving clues together. Fifth, always guessing instead of skipping when confidence is low. Avoid these and your team scores will jump.

walkthrough.title: The Second Thought Rule
walkthrough.text: When you see the mystery word, your first association is probably the same one everyone else has. Do not write it. Think of the second or third thing that comes to mind. A clue that is slightly less obvious but survives duplicate cancellation is infinitely more valuable than the perfect clue that gets cancelled. The best clue-givers consistently avoid the obvious while staying understandable.

walkthrough.title: Stay in the Same Lane
walkthrough.text: When the mystery word has multiple meanings, most people will think of the most common one. Lean toward that meaning unless you are very confident the group will split evenly. If the word is Mercury, more people will think planet than Roman god. Write a planet-related clue that is different from everyone else's planet clue. Do not go off on your own tangent. The guesser needs clues that point in the same direction.

walkthrough.title: Clue Categories That Work
walkthrough.text: There are several types of clues that tend to be both understandable and unique. Category clues tell the guesser what kind of thing the word is. Function clues describe what the thing does. Location clues tell where you find it. Association clues connect to the word through a common phrase. Mix these approaches among your group and you will rarely duplicate.

walkthrough.title: The Guesser's Strategy
walkthrough.text: As the guesser, do not blurt out the first word that comes to mind. Look at all the surviving clues and think about what concept they share. Sometimes one clue alone does not make sense, but combined with another, it clicks. Also think about what clues were cancelled. If two clues were removed, they were probably the most obvious association, which tells you something about the direction of the word.

walkthrough.title: When to Guess Versus Skip
walkthrough.text: Early in the game, take risks. You have twelve cards left after the first turn, so a wrong guess only costs you about fifteen percent of the deck. Late in the game, a wrong guess costs two of your remaining cards, which could be twenty or thirty percent of what is left. As a rule, guess freely in the first half and skip conservatively in the second half.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to Your Group
walkthrough.text: Every group has tendencies. Some groups default to pop culture references. Others think literally. Pay attention to what your teammates write in early rounds and adjust your clue-writing style accordingly. If your group always writes synonyms, switch to function or context clues. If they go obscure, anchor your clues to obvious associations. Adapting to your specific group is the strongest skill in Just One.

walkthrough.title: Choose Familiar Words
walkthrough.text: As the active player, you pick the number from one to five. Glance at the card before placing it in your easel if the rules in your group allow it. Otherwise, call out a number and hope for the best. If your group allows choosing after seeing the words, pick the word that you think most players know well. Obscure words lead to weak clues and more skips.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't write the obvious clue — it'll get duplicated and cancelled
Don't use invalid clues (same word family, translations)
Don't be so clever that nobody understands your clue
Don't guess too quickly — consider all surviving clues together
Don't always guess — skip when confidence is below 50%

summary.title: The Second Thought Rule
bullets: Your first association = everyone's first association = cancelled
Write your 2nd or 3rd thought instead
A surviving mediocre clue beats a cancelled perfect one

summary.title: Stay in the Same Lane
bullets: For ambiguous words, lean toward the most common meaning
Don't go on your own tangent — guesser needs consistent direction
Be different from teammates but in the same conceptual area

summary.title: Clue Categories That Work
bullets: Category (what kind of thing), Function (what it does)
Location (where you find it), Association (common phrase)
Mixing these approaches reduces duplicate risk

summary.title: The Guesser's Strategy
bullets: Don't blurt out first word — analyze all surviving clues together
Cancelled clues = probably the most obvious association
Look for the shared concept across remaining clues

summary.title: When to Guess Versus Skip
bullets: Early game: guess freely (12 cards left = low risk)
Late game: skip conservatively (wrong guess = 2 cards lost)
Rule of thumb: guess first half, skip second half when unsure

summary.title: Adapt to Your Group
bullets: Watch teammates' clue patterns in early rounds
If group writes synonyms → switch to function/context clues
Adapting to your specific group is the strongest skill

summary.title: Choose Familiar Words
bullets: As active player, pick word numbers your group will know
Obscure words → weak clues → more skips/wrong guesses
Common, concrete words get the best results

walkthrough.title: Probability-Based Clue Writing
walkthrough.text: Think about what percentage of your group will write any given clue. If five people are giving clues and you think three will write the same word, do not write it. Instead, write the word you estimate only one person would choose. The math is simple: a clue that survives has infinite more value than one that gets cancelled. Aim to be in the group of one.

walkthrough.title: The Anchor-Plus-Unique Strategy
walkthrough.text: In a team of clue-givers, the ideal distribution is one anchor clue that clearly points to the mystery word plus several unique clues that add specificity. You cannot coordinate, but you can estimate. If you think someone else will write the anchor, go unique. If you think everyone will try to be unique, maybe you should be the anchor. This meta-game of predicting your teammates is where Just One gets deep.

walkthrough.title: Using Cancelled Clues as Information
walkthrough.text: As the guesser, cancelled clues are not invisible. You know that two or more people wrote the same thing, which means the mystery word is strongly associated with whatever got cancelled. If you see three surviving clues and two were cancelled, think about what obvious word would cause two people to duplicate. That pattern can narrow your guess significantly.

walkthrough.title: Difficulty-Based Word Selection
walkthrough.text: As the active player, word difficulty matters enormously. Concrete nouns like Lighthouse or Penguin are easy because they have many strong associations. Abstract concepts like Freedom or Silence are much harder because clue-givers struggle to be both clear and unique. When you have the choice, pick concrete nouns with multiple distinctive features.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Risk Management
walkthrough.text: In the final three or four turns, every card is precious. As a clue-giver, write the clearest possible clue even if it risks duplication. Better to give the guesser one crystal-clear clue than two obscure ones. As the guesser, skip anything below seventy percent confidence. A wrong guess in the final turns is devastating because it removes the current card, the next deck card, and potentially a previously successful card.

walkthrough.title: The Group Meta-Game
walkthrough.text: Over multiple games, your group develops patterns. Some players always write synonyms. Others always go for pop culture. Use this knowledge. If you know Player 3 always writes the obvious clue, you are free to go unique. If Player 5 is always obscure, you might need to anchor. The best Just One teams develop an unspoken division of labor where each person naturally fills a different clue niche.

walkthrough.title: Compound Clue Tricks
walkthrough.text: While you can only write one word, certain words encode more information than others. A proper noun like Shakespeare tells the guesser both the category and a specific association. A hyphenated word that your group accepts, like ice-cream, can be more specific than either word alone. Maximize the information density of your single word.

walkthrough.title: Score Threshold Awareness
walkthrough.text: Know the scoring thresholds. Thirteen is perfect, twelve is incredible, eleven is awesome. These benchmarks help your group calibrate risk. If you have nine successes with three cards remaining, you need all three to reach twelve. That might be worth taking risks. If you already have eleven with two left, playing it safe to protect your eleven might be smarter than risking it for thirteen.

summary.title: Probability-Based Clue Writing
bullets: Estimate how many teammates will write any given clue
If 3 of 5 will write it → don't write it
Aim to be the only person who writes your clue

summary.title: The Anchor-Plus-Unique Strategy
bullets: Ideal: 1 anchor clue (clear) + several unique clues (specific)
If someone else will anchor → go unique
If everyone goes unique → be the anchor

summary.title: Using Cancelled Clues as Information
bullets: Cancelled clues = strong associations with the mystery word
Guesser can infer what the obvious word was
Pattern narrows the guess even without seeing the cancelled clues

summary.title: Difficulty-Based Word Selection
bullets: Concrete nouns (Lighthouse, Penguin) are easiest
Abstract concepts (Freedom, Silence) are hardest
Pick words with multiple distinctive features

summary.title: Late-Game Risk Management
bullets: Clue-givers: prioritize clarity over uniqueness in final turns
Guesser: skip below 70% confidence in final turns
Wrong final guess = lose current + deck + previously successful card

summary.title: The Group Meta-Game
bullets: Over multiple games, learn each player's clue-writing tendencies
Use knowledge of teammates to fill different clue niches
Best teams develop unspoken division of labor

summary.title: Compound Clue Tricks
bullets: Some single words encode more info (proper nouns, compounds)
Maximize information density in your 1 allowed word
Shakespeare > play (tells category AND association)

summary.title: Score Threshold Awareness
bullets: Know thresholds: 13 perfect, 12 incredible, 11 awesome
Calibrate risk based on current score vs. cards remaining
Protect a great score vs. gamble for perfect

entries.definition: The player who guesses the mystery word each turn. They draw a card, choose a number, and then see only the surviving clues after duplicate cancellation.
Every player who is not the active player. They write one-word clues secretly to help the active player guess the mystery word.
The target word that the active player must guess. Selected by the active player calling a number from 1 to 5 on the drawn card.
A reusable writing stand used to display clues and hold the mystery word card. Each player gets one easel (two in a 3-player game).
A set of exactly 13 word cards drawn from the full 110-card deck. This is the entire game for one session.
When two or more clue-givers write the same word or close variants (plurals, gender forms, spelling differences), all matching clues are cancelled and hidden from the guesser.
A clue that violates the rules: the mystery word in a different form, a translation, a same-family word, or an invented word. Invalid clues are cancelled.
The active player may choose not to guess. Skipping loses only the current card (1 card penalty) instead of the 2-card penalty for a wrong guess.
A wrong guess removes the current card AND the top card of the deck. On the final turn, it also removes one previously successful card.
When every clue is cancelled due to duplicates or invalidity, the active player cannot guess. The current card is removed with no additional penalty.
Words derived from the same root. For example, if the mystery word is 'swim,' the clues 'swimming,' 'swimmer,' and 'swam' are all invalid same-family clues.
13 = perfect, 12 = incredible, 11 = awesome, 9-10 = great, 7-8 = average, 4-6 = good start, 0-3 = try again.
In a 3-player game, each player takes 2 easels and writes 2 clues per turn. Duplicate cancellation still applies across all clues.
On the last card of the game, a wrong guess also removes one previously successful card from the score pile, in addition to the standard 2-card penalty.
A clear, direct clue that strongly points to the mystery word. Risks duplication but is highly useful if it survives. Best when only one person writes it.
